nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and frames it as a congenital/inherited cause of chronic anemia.
Explains why anemia can be present despite an inappropriately low or only mildly elevated reticulocyte response (must use the term reticulocyte) due to ineffective erythropoiesis.
Uses patient-appropriate language and checks understanding (e.g., asks the patient to repeat key points or invites questions).

Communicates that confirmation typically involves hematology evaluation and specialized testing (e.g., peripheral smear patterns, bone marrow morphology, genetic testing) without overpromising certainty if not yet confirmed.

Mentions key complications to monitor (iron overload even without many transfusions, gallstones/splenomegaly) and provides safety-net advice for worsening fatigue, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, fever, or jaundice.
If any medication is proposed, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical), contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (noncritical).
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis by including both required terms: congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and reticulocyte.

Meets communication milestone explain_treatment by discussing folic acid and transfusion as treatment concepts (even if not prescribed in this L1 task), including when transfusion might be needed (symptomatic or severe anemia) and that folic acid supports red cell production.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize CDA as the most likely diagnosis based on chronic anemia with ineffective erythropoiesis features and document it; provide clear explanation including reticulocyte concept and basic treatment overview (folic acid, transfusion).

family history of anemia or consanguinity
splenomegaly symptoms or known enlarged spleen
steps: Elicit anemia history (onset, severity, symptoms) and relevant family history.
Assess for hemolysis/ineffective erythropoiesis clues (jaundice, gallstones, splenomegaly) and transfusion history/iron overload risk.
Explain that congenital dyserythropoietic anemia is an inherited disorder where the bone marrow makes abnormal red cells that often die before entering circulation, leading to anemia.
Explain reticulocyte expectations and that the reticulocyte response may be inappropriately low/only modest for the degree of anemia due to ineffective erythropoiesis.

Briefly outline management principles: folic acid supplementation support; transfusion when clinically indicated; monitoring for iron overload and other complications; hematology involvement for confirmation and longitudinal care.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents CDA as a probable diagnosis while explicitly noting it is pending confirmation and advises hematology follow-up and monitoring for iron overload; still includes required diagnosis and reticulocyte explanation and mentions folic acid and transfusion.
steps: Summarize findings consistent with congenital dyserythropoietic anemia and state it as likely/probable pending confirmatory testing.
Explain ineffective erythropoiesis and the reticulocyte response in plain language.
Discuss treatment concepts (folic acid, transfusion) and complication surveillance (iron overload, gallstones, splenomegaly).
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (probable).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent provides a brief differential in conversation (without using record_differential tool) but still anchors on CDA and completes the required diagnosis recording.
steps: Consider and verbally contrast other causes of chronic anemia (e.g., thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, autoimmune hemolysis) and explain why CDA best fits the pattern of ineffective erythropoiesis/reticulocyte response.

Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.

general_concerns: Believes the condition is mainly a 'willpower problem' and that strict dieting alone should fix the constant hunger.
Thinks the genetic point mutation might have been caused by something her mother did during pregnancy (e.g., stress or eating the wrong foods).
Assumes that because this is 'not classic Prader-Willi,' she won’t have long-term risks like diabetes, sleep apnea, or low bone density.
Believes growth hormone treatment is basically a steroid that will 'damage the heart' or cause cancer if used too long.
Thinks skipping meals is safer than planned meals because 'any calories turn straight into fat' for her.
Believes exercise has to be intense to help, and that walking or light strength training is pointless.
economic_concerns: Worries insurance won’t cover genetic testing follow-up, nutrition counseling, or sleep studies because the diagnosis is 'rare' or 'not exact PWS.'
Assumes medications or hormone therapy will be unaffordable long-term and may need to be stopped once she turns 21 and loses certain benefits.
Believes healthier food options and structured programs are only for people with high income, so change isn’t realistic for her household.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious, vague speaker. Start with: “I can’t stop thinking about food and I’m gaining weight even though I try not to.” Volunteer only: always hungry/never full, rapid weight gain over the last year, and you get very upset/anxious when meal routines change. If asked about eating behavior, admit you eat very fast, keep searching for more after big meals, sometimes eat in secret, and have had episodes of stomach pain after overeating. If asked about sleep/energy, report daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, and waking up gasping. If asked about mental/skin/learning, mention skin picking with scabs when stressed, lifelong learning problems with school supports, low muscle/weakness. If asked about hormones/sexual health, say low interest in sex and irregular periods. Do not mention low sweating, low pain sensation, constipation, small hands/feet, or feeling cold unless directly asked. Resist admitting stealing food/lying/vomiting; only disclose if the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks directly. Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid/Cushing’s/depression. Emphasize cost/insurance fears. If met with empathy, soften, give slightly more detail, and ask if help is affordable.

Possible obstructive sleep apnea causing daytime somnolence and worsening metabolic profile
Neurodevelopmental/behavioral issues (autistic traits, rigidity) complicating dietary management and history reliability

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates evidence-aligned pharmacotherapy option (GLP-1 agonist) as an adjunct to strict food security and weight management, with safety checks prior to prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "Prader-Willi-like syndrome" and "genetic mutation" and clarifies that this is a genetic condition with characteristic hyperphagia/obesity risk.

Explains treatment and must include both "food security plan" (environmental control of access to food) and "GLP-1 agonist" as an adjunct option; sets expectations that medication is not a substitute for supervision/structure.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: explicitly asks about medication allergies (critical allergy_check) and screens for contraindications to GLP-1 receptor agonists (critical contraindication_check), including personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN2, prior pancreatitis, severe GI disease/gastroparesis, pregnancy where relevant, and ability to maintain hydration/monitoring.

If prescribing, addresses non-critical drug_interaction_check by reviewing current meds/supplements and flags additive hypoglycemia risk if combined with insulin/sulfonylureas (if present), with a plan for monitoring/adjustment.

Provides a clear follow-up/monitoring plan in narrative (even if not tool-recorded): weight/BMI trajectory, appetite/hyperphagia behaviors, GI side effects, hydration, glucose/A1c if indicated, and red flags (persistent severe abdominal pain/vomiting).

Uses patient-centered communication: confirms understanding, invites questions, and discusses shared decision-making and realistic goals (weight stabilization/reduction, reduced hyperphagia-driven behaviors).

Avoids unsafe claims: does not present GLP-1 agonists as curative; does not recommend unproven appetite suppressants or stimulants without clear indication; avoids extreme caloric restriction without supervision.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to point mutation, implement strict food security plan, and prescribe a GLP-1 receptor agonist after allergy and contraindication screening.
must_collect: personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2
GI symptoms suggesting gastroparesis or severe GI disease
steps: Confirm key features consistent with Prader-Willi-like phenotype and document diagnosis as due to point mutation.
Explain genetic basis and expected clinical issues (hyperphagia, obesity risk, behavioral concerns) in plain language.
Recommend a food security plan: controlled access to food, consistent routines, caregiver alignment, and environmental modifications.
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check and contraindication_check for GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy; review meds for interactions and hypoglycemia risk if combined with insulin/sulfonylureas.
Prescribe GLP-1 receptor agonist with counseling on titration, common adverse effects, and urgent warning symptoms; set measurable goals.
Outline follow-up timing and monitoring parameters (weight/appetite, GI tolerance, metabolic markers as appropriate).

acceptable_variants.description: Same core plan, but agent emphasizes non-pharmacologic management first and still offers GLP-1 agonist as adjunct after safety checks and shared decision-making.
